data.title: Buy The Dip: 6%-Yielding Inflation Hedges Getting Way Too Cheap
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell recently said that the Trump administration's tariff policies are likely going to complicate the Fed's efforts to keep inflation down while also striving for full employment, as its dual mandate requires. This is because tariffs will likely increase the cost of a lot of imports, thereby driving up inflation, while also weighing on the economy due to the uncertainties and disruptions to normal supply chains. This in turn will complicate matters for the Fed because if inflation bounces back due to the tariffs, the Fed may be inclined to want to hike interest rates. However, if the economy is also weakening due to the disruptions caused by the tariffs, the Fed will not want to hike rates but instead cut them to prop up the economy. It will therefore likely be caught between a rock and a hard place when it comes to trying to fulfill its dual mandate.

With this in mind, now is a good time for investors to start thinking about playing defense while also ensuring your portfolio is adequately hedged against inflation. One obvious option is gold (GLD) and gold-related companies like Newmont Corporation (NEM). However, gold and NEM have both been on a tear recently, meaning that they are no longer the compelling values that they once were. On top of that, neither of them pay much, if any, yield. So for investors who prioritize passive income from dividends, they really are not ideal investment opportunities. Instead, investors can look for real asset inflation protection elsewhere while generating an attractive yield. In this article, I will discuss two options that currently yield about 6% each that are deeply undervalued after a recent sell-off.

Opportunity #1

The first option that they can turn to is the Gladstone Land Corporation (LAND), which is a farmland REIT that trades at a steep discount to its net asset value, despite trading at a premium to its NAV on average over the past five years. Additionally, due to the recent sell-off in its stock price, the yield has reached about 6%, which is far above its historical average.

Meanwhile, its underlying portfolio is well diversified by crop type and geography.

The company also has a good track record of sustaining and even growing its dividend over time, especially when compared to other farmland REITs like Farmland Partners (FPI).

While the underlying portfolio and balance sheet are quite solid with well-staggered debt (weighted average remaining term to maturity of 8.0 years) and lease (weighted average remaining lease term of 5.1 years) maturities, the company also has been reducing its cash flow burden by repurchasing its preferred stock at a deep discount to par value, thereby providing even better support for its common dividend.

Last but not least, insiders are well aligned with shareholders due to management owning 9% of the common stock.

As a result, investors can earn a very attractive current yield backed by a well-diversified portfolio of a very defensive and inflation-resistant asset class with well-aligned management and a solid balance sheet and lease expiration set up. Best of all, this is on sale at a 33% discount to private market values right now.

Opportunity #2

Another attractive option for investors to earn a high yield from an inflation hedge is via Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). CNQ is an energy production stock that should serve as a good inflation hedge because energy has historically delivered the strongest real returns across assets whenever inflation has been high. On top of that, CNQ has well-diversified energy exposure as its 2025 budget production mix has 27% coming from natural gas, 26% from heavy oil, 11% from light oil and NGLs, and 36% from SCO.

CNQ also has a lengthy production profile with the most approved developed and total approved reserves among its Canadian energy peers with a 33-year total approved reserve life index and has the largest crude oil reserves as well as the largest natural gas reserves in Canada. Furthermore, when compared to global energy peers, it has the second-largest total reserves. CNQ also has some of the lowest-cost energy production assets in the world, which means that it should continue to generate free cash flow and pay out its dividend even at materially lower energy prices. This gives it significant durability through all sorts of economic cycles, which explains why it has grown its dividend for 25 straight years on top of substantial share purchases.

In addition, it has a very strong balance sheet with investment-grade credit ratings from both Moody's and S&P, and it has $4.7 billion in liquidity with a very reasonable debt to equity ratio of 1.1 times. When you add all that up together with its roughly 6% dividend yield, which is quite high compared to its historic average, and its very cheap price to net asset value of 0.79 times—despite trading at over 1 times net asset value on numerous occasions throughout its history and trading on average in line with its net asset value throughout its history—CNQ offers investors a very attractive combination of current yield, dividend growth, share buybacks, conservative balance sheet, a deep and high-quality low-cost production profile, and a significant return on investment.

Coming from a company that focuses on a commodity that has proven to be one of the best at inflation hedges, CNQ is a great buy-the-dip opportunity for inflation-averse investors.

Investor Takeaway

While inflation may very possibly rebound in the coming months due to disruptions from the Trump administration's tariffs, investors have a good opportunity today to dig in and hedge against diverse outcomes by buying high-quality, well-managed inflation hedges for pennies on the dollar while also generating a 6% dividend yield from each of them. With LAND and CNQ, investors can both boost their passive income stream and play defense against the corrosive power of inflation by buying the dip.

These are the sorts of opportunities we are loading up on at High Yield Investor.

data.title: 3 Dividend Kings To Buy On Sale Now
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Kody Kester.

The broader markets have been quite volatile through the first three months and counting of 2025.

Recession and trade war concerns have led the S&P 500 index (SP500) index to fall 13% from its all-time highs and briefly dipped into bear market territory. The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is currently 18% off its all-time highs and was also in bear market territory this week. For an idea of just how much fear, uncertainty, and doubt is currently present in the market, CNN's Fear & Greed Index is currently at 18 - extreme fear territory.

In this environment, it's more important than ever to remain confident in a long-term approach. For me, the dividend growth investing strategy is my approach. Over the past eight years, it has helped me to remain calm (as calm as one can be in periods of turbulence) through two bear markets and several market corrections.

In the current landscape, this will remain my strategy. Buy top-notch dividend growth stocks at clearance valuations. Well, Dividend Kings are at the top of the heap in terms of consistency in the investing universe. They're not perfect and could experience a fall from grace. Yet, for at least half a century, they've returned more and more cash to shareholders.

Let's take a look at three undervalued Dividend Kings that could be smart buys.

PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP): Snack On This Buying Opportunity

At the risk of being Captain Obvious, PepsiCo's brand portfolio is the envy of just about every other company on the planet. From top to bottom, the company's product portfolio has billion-dollar brands. That's how PepsiCo generated almost $92 billion in net revenue in 2024. Of that amount, 58% ($53 billion) came from convenient foods. These include the Lay's, Cheetos, and Doritos brands. Beverages contributed the remaining 42% of net revenue ($39 billion). These include the eponymous Pepsi soft drink and Gatorade sports drink brands.

Doing business in just about every country and territory in the world, PepsiCo seems to be a truly global business. With that being said, it's not quite as global as one would think. What do I mean?

Well, North America represents merely 5% of the global population. Yet, it comprised about 60% of PepsiCo's 2024 net revenue mix. The remaining 95% of the global population was just 40% of the company's net revenue mix. This presents the company with a lengthy international growth runway. That's important to note because the five-year CAGR of organic revenue in international markets is 10%. For context, this is about 300 basis points better than the North American market.

Put another way, PepsiCo has plenty of room to grow into what's already a $1.3 trillion and counting total addressable market for global beverages and convenient foods. It's anticipated that these categories are going to compound by the mid-single-digits annually in the years to come.

That's why the company believes it can generate organic revenue growth of 4% to 6% annually. As PepsiCo further invests in annual productivity initiatives like automation and optimization, it thinks its core operating margin can expand by 20 to 30 basis points. Add in modest share repurchases and this can realistically get the company back to high-single-digit annual core constant currency EPS growth.

Beyond the near-term headwinds for 2025 that I discussed last month, analysts are still reasonably confident in PepsiCo's growth outlook. The FAST Graphs analyst consensus is that core EPS will rise by 7% in 2026 to $8.83 (after a 1.5% increase in 2025). Another 6.9% jump in core EPS to $9.44 is being predicted for 2027.

This type of growth doesn't jump off the page. However, it gets the job done considering another aspect of PepsiCo: The market-topping dividend income. The company's 3.9% forward dividend yield dwarfs the S&P 500 index's 1.4% yield. PepsiCo's payout is also adequately covered by profits, with the core EPS payout ratio likely to register in the high-60% range in 2025.

If that wasn't enough, the company has spectacular financial strength going for it, too. PepsiCo's interest coverage ratio in 2024 was 14. That's enough for S&P to award an A+ credit rating on a stable outlook.

PepsiCo's valuation is what seals the deal for me and is why I added to my position in February. The company's forward 12-month P/E ratio is just 17.4 (accounting for a 77% weight for 2025 and 23% weight for 2026). This is below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 23 and my fair value multiple of 20.7. That suggests PepsiCo's shares are worth $174 apiece. From the current $147 share price (as of April 10th, 2025), this would be a 16% discount to fair value. If PepsiCo meets growth expectations and returns to my fair value P/E ratio, it could post 15% annual total returns through 2027 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details were according to PepsiCo's CAGNY 2025 Presentation).

Black Hills Corporation (NYSE:BKH): A Quality Utility In Economically Thriving Areas

Black Hills Corporation is a regulated utility with roughly 9,200 miles of electric lines and 49,200 miles of natural gas lines. Operating in eight states, the company serves nearly 1.4 million customers.

As would be expected for having operations throughout the United States, BKH is also quite geographically diversified. The company's biggest market of South Dakota accounted for just 18% of its estimated utility rate base as of Dec. 31, 2024.

BKH's diversified business mix doesn't end there, either. The company's gas rate base made up approximately 57% of its total rate base, with the remaining 43% being derived from its electric businesses.

Expanding a bit on BKH's geographic profile from earlier, the company operates in exceptionally vibrant service territories. In the past five years, the regulated utility's annual customer count has compounded by 1.1%. This is roughly triple the BKH states and U.S. averages of 0.4%, respectively.

BKH expects its service territories to continue to thrive. That explains why it upped its five-year capital spending plan by about 10% to $4.7 billion for 2025 through 2029. This capex should lead to a steady rate base growth over the medium term, which supports BKH's annual diluted EPS growth target of 4% to 6%.

The FAST Graphs analyst consensus appears to buy into the company's growth prospects as well. The reduced dependency on any one given market gives me confidence that these estimates are well-founded. In 2025, analysts are predicting a 4.9% increase in diluted EPS to $4.10. Another 5.4% rise in diluted EPS to $4.32 is being forecasted for 2026. In 2027, an additional 6% jump in diluted EPS to $4.58 is being prognosticated.

In terms of execution, it's quite likely that BKH can pull off its capital spending plans. That's because the company's debt-to-capital ratio was 55% at the end of 2024. This warrants a BBB+ credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

BKH also offers a generous 4.6% dividend yield. This is backed up by a payout ratio that's positioned to be in the upper 60% range. That gives BKH plenty of room to grow the payout further.

BKH's valuation is also reasonably attractive. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 13.4, which is below the 10-year normal P/E ratio of 17.4. Compared to my fair value multiple of 17.4 and fair value of $72 a share, BKH could be trading at a 20% discount to fair value. If the utility reverts to fair value and matches growth expectations, it could generate 16% annual total returns by the end of 2027 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details were according to BKH's March 2025 Investor Presentation).

Emerson Electric (NYSE:EMR): A Great Bet On Powerful Economic Trends

As a global industrial technology and software leader, Emerson Electric's importance to the overall economy is hard to overstate. All the 25 top life sciences companies rely on its software, control systems, and intelligent devices to run their daily operations. In renewables, 65,000 wind turbines run on Emerson's systems. Nine of the top 10 semiconductor manufacturers use the company's intelligent devices and controls. Approximately 50% of North American and 20% of global power generation is automated using Emerson's control systems and software.

The investment thesis for Emerson revolves around numerous secular growth trends playing out globally. These include digital transformation, decarbonization, and nearshoring. As industries like renewables, hydrogen, life sciences, and semiconductors continue to grow, the demand for Emerson's innovative solutions should also rise over time.

That's behind the company's through-the-cycle target of 4% to 7% organic revenue growth. As the company focuses on operational excellence, this should also help the adjusted EBITDA margin further expand. Along with share repurchases, this is Emerson's path to delivering double-digit annual adjusted EPS growth over the long haul.

The company's 2.1% dividend yield isn't as high as our other two picks. However, it doesn't have to be with the level of growth of which Emerson is capable. The company's payout ratio is primed to be in the mid-30% range in FY 2025, which provides it with the flexibility to build on its status as a Dividend King.

Emerson is also a financial fortress. The company's interest coverage ratio in FY 2024 was 12.5. This justifies an A credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

Priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 16.5, Emerson’s shares also appear to be trading at an appealing valuation. That's well below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 19.2. The current $101 share price is 16% less than my fair value estimate of $120. If Emerson returns to fair value and lives up to the growth consensus, it could be set up for 13% annual total returns through FY 2027 (unless otherwise noted, all information was from Emerson's March 2025 Overview and Emerson's 2024 Annual Report).

Summary: Great Buy And Hold Candidates For The Long Haul

PEP, BKH, and EMR should each deliver respectable growth in the years ahead. This comes on top of an equal-weighted average 3.5% dividend yield, which is almost triple the S&P 500 index's 1.4% yield. The market-beating income generated by these companies is also secure, with the average payout ratio coming in comfortably below the industry-safe guidelines set forth by rating agencies. The underlying quality of these picks is further supported by the A-rated balance sheet average.

Best of all, the companies appear to be trading at double-digit percentage discounts to fair value. That should lead to 13% to 16% annual total returns for each stock through 2027. Thus, my bullishness toward the trio.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Analysts Weigh Meta's Future Without Instagram, WhatsApp
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

SA Asks: How would META fare if it lost Instagram, WhatsApp? (00:25) China bans use of 'smart driving' terms in auto ads. (02:56) McDonald's (MCD) is bringing back its popular Snack Wrap menu item this year. (03:54)

This is an abridged transcript.

Less than a week into a trial that is expected to last up to two months, analysts are weighing what could happen to Meta Platforms if forced to divest Instagram and WhatsApp.

Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is currently embroiled in antitrust litigation with the FTC over its takeovers of Instagram and WhatsApp, which it acquired in 2012 and 2014, respectively.

Opening arguments in the case were presented before a federal court on Monday. If Meta (NASDAQ:META) loses, it may be ordered to divest itself of one or both of the hugely popular services.

How would Meta (META) fare without Instagram and/or WhatsApp? Seeking Alpha analysts UFD Capital and Nexus Research weigh in.

UFD Capital: We believe that Meta (META) could survive losing Instagram and/or WhatsApp. These days, Meta (META) functions more like a holding company, and it's our opinion that much of the value they generate over the coming years will be from new initiatives rather than iterations on their existing platforms. Losing one or more of these platforms will be detrimental, but probably not the end of the world.

What is less certain is whether Instagram and WhatsApp would thrive on their own. Instagram can stand alone just fine, but will be less efficient at serving ads without cross-platform data from Meta (META). Despite a massive user base, WhatsApp monetization has been relatively slow. WhatsApp represents a potential opportunity for Meta (META); however, the app may not be able to monetize very well on its own.

We believe that whatever the outcome of the FTC case is, investors will not suffer too much over the long term. A potential upside scenario is the possibility that the sum of the parts is worth more than the whole, but for a tech company of this nature that is unlikely.

Nexus Research: It would be a major blow to the company, given how much Instagram contributes to Meta’s (META) total revenue (48.4% in 2024), and how WhatsApp is currently the most promising channel for “Meta AI” usage and automated business messaging (or “Business AIs”).

The possibility of Meta (META) having to break off Instagram and WhatsApp is likely to weigh on stock price performance going forward. And that is on top of the fact that President Trump is keen to sustain the operations of TikTok in the U.S., throwing cold water on the bullish narrative that Meta (META) could benefit from the ban of the Chinese social media app. The Trump administration’s regulatory stance is certainly not in Meta’s (META) favor at the moment.

China has implemented a ban on the use of terms such as "smart driving" and "autonomous driving" in automotive advertisements.

The move follows a fatal accident involving a Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) (OTCPK:XIACY) SU7 sedan and is part of a broader crackdown of advanced driving assistance systems and related technologies.

Automakers are prohibited from using marketing terms like "smart driving," "autonomous driving," "automatic driving," "intelligent driving," or "advanced intelligent driving" in promotional materials. Instead, manufacturers must use precise terminology, such as "L(number) assisted driving," which corresponds to the internationally recognized levels of driving automation.

The mandate on vehicle advertising was delivered by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology on Wednesday. The revised rule also prohibits automakers from testing and enhancing their ADAS through remote software upgrades for cars that have already been delivered to customers.

McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) plans to bring back its popular Snack Wrap menu item sometime this year,

In a cryptic social media post, the restaurant chain teased that the launch would be on the 14th, but did not specify which month.

The Snack Wrap, which has been off the McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) menu for almost a decade, was first introduced in the United States in 2006 and quickly became a fan favorite.

Over the last five years, various social media posts asking McDonald's (MCD) to bring back Snack Wrap have gone viral.

Despite the popularity of Snack Wrap, the restaurant chain discontinued it in 2016 due mainly to operational inefficiencies. The assembly process was considered too time-consuming for the chain’s fast-paced kitchens, and the wrap ultimately didn’t meet internal sales expectations.

The revived Snack Wrap is expected to feature some updates.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Alcoa expects $90M hit from tariffs in Q2; CEO says impractical to boost capacity in U.S.

CBP says $500 million collected in reciprocal tariffs since April 5

Taiwan Semiconductor in charts: 3nm, 5nm, 7nm, 16nm and 28nm chips power Q1 revenue growth

Catalyst watch:

  • Shareholders with Playa Hotels & Resorts (PLYA) will vote on the proposed merger with Hyatt Hotels (H).

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 1% at $63/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.5% at $84,000. Gold is down 0.8% at $3,322.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is down 0.6% and the DAX is flat.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) +2.5% – Shares edged higher after Redburn upgraded the database software provider to Neutral from Sell, citing limited downside risk.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index

  • 10:30 am EIA Natural Gas Report

data.title: Simon Property Group: Price Drop Is A Major Opportunity For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Introduction

Before the tariff turmoil in recent weeks, Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) had been on a nice share price rally.

As a result of market volatility, their share price hit a new 52-week low of $136.34 at the time of writing.

But due to the 90-day pause on tariffs, SPG rallied to a price of $153 before pulling back to $148 where they trade at the time of writing.

Despite the rally, the REIT is still attractive, especially for long-term dividend investors.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, full-year performance, and why Simon Property Group's price drop is a massive buying opportunity for the long term.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Simon Property Group this past December in an article titled: Still An Opportunistic Buy For Long-Term Investors. Since then, their share price has declined double-digits, more so than the S&P's 10.16% at the time of writing.

Although SPG enjoyed a 39.27% increase through their first 3 quarters, the REIT was still attractively valued with a forward P/FFO multiple of 14.14x.

Their price target of $204 using their peer median of 15.87x was also attractive, as this offered investors double-digit upside. The REIT rallied to roughly $190 in early March before pulling back as the market entered correction territory.

Q4 Earnings & Full-year Performance

Despite a tough macro environment that has weighed on REITs and subsequently weighing on consumer spending, SPG delivered a solid performance, although they saw a $0.07 miss on their bottom line during Q4 earnings back in February.

FFO amounted to $3.35 while their top line managed to beat analysts' estimates by a solid $170 million. Both FFO & revenue grew from the previous quarter's $3.05 and $1.48 billion, respectively.

On an annualized basis, both also grew 3.7% from $3.23 and 3.3% from $1.53 billion. Again, solid considering tighter, but resilient consumer spending despite higher interest rates.

This is a reflection of Simon's high-end stores in their premium malls, as well as their locations in affluent areas. For the full-year, their FFO managed to see growth of 3.9% from the previous year.

This was a result of their solid 4.6% portfolio NOI growth for the year. For comparison, Regency Centers Corp. (REG) saw 3.6% growth over the same period.

During the quarter, they signed 1,500 leases totaling 6.1 million square feet and a record 5,500 for the year.

Their occupancy also increased year-over-year 70 basis points to a year-end 96.5%. Peer Kimco Realty Corp.'s (KIM) occupancy rose 10 basis points year-over-year to 96.3%.

Why SPG Looks Poised For Growth

Aside from their strong fundamentals and premium malls in affluent locations, the premium outlet REIT continues to expand, particularly internationally. They acquired two premium outlets in Italy and opened an outlet in Indonesia's capital, Jakarta, in late March.

Domestically, SPG has also been busy executing. First, opening a premium outlet in Tulsa, Oklahoma and another outlet expected in one of the fastest-growing cities, Nashville.

This is expected to start construction next year and projected to be 325,000 square feet. They also anticipate starting construction on 4 to 5 mixed-use projects this year as well.

2025 Outlook

Although growth is expected to slow this year, FFO is still projected in a range of $12.40 - $12.65, a 2.3% growth rate at midpoint. Domestic NOI growth is expected to be 3%.

Their combined JCPenney & SPARC Group brands, Catalyst Brands, is expected to positively impact EBITDA this year as well. So, from a long-term standpoint, Simon Property Group looks poised to see growth and potential upside.

A-Rated Balance Sheet

Despite a potential recession that could cause a decline of foot traffic as this would likely impact consumer discretionary income, SPG's A-rated balance sheet positions them favorably.

They also have robust liquidity available with $10.1 billion, and $2 billion in cash. Their net debt to EBITDA was 5.2x. On top of this, their free cash flow after paying dividends was strong, with $1.5 billion available.

Dividend Safety

Simon Property Group often gets a bad rap since they were forced to cut their dividend during the pandemic. But the REIT has quickly gotten back to growing their dividend, with this now at pre-pandemic levels.

SPG declared a $2.10 dividend, a 7.7% increase from 2023. Moreover, they ended the year with a conservative 62% FFO payout ratio.

This gives them ample room for flexibility should they suffer a slowdown due to a recession. If one does come to fruition, I expect SPG to continue paying and raising the dividend.

> 25% Upside

As stated earlier, at the time of writing, Simon Property Group made a new 52-week low before rallying to a share price of $148, where they currently trade.

Using their midpoint of guidance, this gives them a forward P/FFO multiple of 11.82x, a low multiple for a REIT of SPG's quality.

This is slightly higher than Kimco Realty's 11.79x and above the peer median of 11.70x. However, SPG deserves to trade at a premium. The REIT was also recently upgraded as a result of their undervaluation.

And despite the upside being potentially limited due to fears of a recession, their well-covered dividend, expansion, and strong fundamentals make them a buy for long-term dividend investors.

Using their normal multiple, SPG offers solid upside, with an end of 2026 price target of $186. However, if interest rates are much lower by then, which I expect, I can see the REIT moving past the $200 mark.

Bottom Line

With market volatility increasing in recent weeks due to tariffs and recession fears, many quality stocks are now in oversold territory, dropping to new 52-week lows.

As a result, this has created attractive buying opportunities, especially for long-term dividend investors. And Simon Property Group fits that bill, trading at an 11.82x forward multiple.

At their current price with more than 25% upside potential, Simon Property Group's valuation along with their A-rated balance sheet and well-covered dividend is worth buying here despite increased recession risks, which could limit upside potential in the near term to medium term.

data.title: Potential Impact Of U.S. Tariffs: Private Equity Manager Perspectives
data.description:
By Peter von Lehe

We surveyed our deep network of private equity managers for insights on the impacts of President Trump’s tariff announcements

Our paper presents key findings from our survey of more than 100 private equity managers worldwide. Insights include:

  • Expected implications on portfolio company revenue, operating costs and EBITDA
  • Potential impacts by sector
  • Mitigation strategies that private equity-backed companies are considering to safeguard and enhance portfolio value

We believe these findings highlight the general resilience of private equity portfolios, as well as the measures that experienced managers are taking to navigate these uncertain times.

Survey Overview

While the market environment remains fluid given the introduction and subsequent postponement of certain announced tariffs by President Trump in early April, we wish to share a few insights on the potential direct impact of tariffs on the private equity industry gathered from our robust network of private equity partners. Neuberger Berman Private Markets leveraged its deep relationships to conduct a survey and gain a holistic view of how private equity managers believed potential tariffs could impact their portfolio companies. Our survey was conducted following the proposed tariffs by President Trump, but prior to the announcement of the 90-day hold. Below we highlight the findings from more than 100 global leading private equity managers invested in thousands of companies. This group of managers is diversified across sub asset class (figure 1), as well as by industry and geography. Notably, approximately 75% of respondents noted that they felt moderately or very confident in predicting the impact on their portfolio companies.

The insights from the survey respondents provide valuable guidance on the resilience of private equity portfolios and the measures being taken to protect and enhance value during this period of uncertainty. The survey findings provide perspectives on the sectors most vulnerable to tariff-related disruptions, as well as the strategies private equity-backed companies are considering to potentially mitigate risks.

Figure 1: Survey Respondent Demographics By Fund Strategy Type

The Private Equity Industry Is Less Exposed to Tariff-Sensitive Sectors Compared to Public Equity Indices

We believe that private equity portfolios are typically less directly exposed to tariff-sensitive sectors and businesses than the economy as a whole or major public equity indices. Private equity managers have historically had less exposure to sectors that rely on imports— such as consumer goods, capital goods and transportation and technology hardware and equipment and semiconductors—and greater exposure to sectors focused on services and intellectual capital, such as software and tech services, healthcare services, commercial and professional services, and communication services sectors. As shown in figure 2, 49% of private equity companies are in these service-oriented sectors, compared to only 27% of companies in the S&P 500.

Figure 2: Exposure To Service Businesses

In our view, this positioning could help buffer private equity from some of the input-cost fluctuations and supply-chain disruptions that tariffs may cause. However, we recognize that there are indirect effects, and any macroeconomic disruptions could affect private equity, as well.

For more information on private equity versus GDP exposure to economic sectors reliant on imports, view our recent insight Tariffs Are Here: What Does That Mean for Private Equity?

Expected Tariff Impact on Revenue, Cost and EBITDA

We asked each survey respondent about the expected impact of tariffs on their portfolio companies’ revenue, operating costs and EBITDA. As shown in figure 3, the results were similar across all three metrics, with only 2% – 4% of respondents anticipating “significant” tariff-related impacts and 45% – 53% indicating the expectation of a “moderate” impact. Notably, 46% – 51% of respondents indicated “no” expected impact. We believe this expected resilience is a combination of fewer tariff-sensitive businesses, the potential implementation of corrective measures to counter tariff effects and the respondents’ focus on direct tariff impacts versus the second-order negative effect from a possible tariff-induced economic slowdown or recession.

Figure 3: Expected Tariff Impacts On Revenue, Cost And EBITDA—PE Manager Survey Data

We further asked the managers to provide the expected tariff impact per industry in which they invest. Figure 4 shows the expected impact on EBITDA for each industry. As we expected, the consumer and industrials sectors are expected to be more heavily impacted (with certain private equity managers expecting “significant” impacts) than services-focused businesses like financial services or healthcare services.

Figure 4: Expected Tariff Impacts On Portfolio Company EBITDA By Industry—PE Manager Survey Data

Private Equity-backed Companies’ Mitigation Actions Include Increasing Price, Diversifying Suppliers/Shifting Sourcing to Non-tariffed Countries, and Reducing Operational Expenses or Other Costs

Private equity is an active asset class and fund managers have the advantage of directly engaging and controlling the companies in which they are invested. This level of influence and ability to act quickly is particularly valuable in times of stress or dislocation.

Historical examples, such as the responses to COVID-19 and the 2008 Financial Crisis, showcase the industry’s proactive approach to navigating challenges. During the pandemic, private equity managers swiftly addressed issues like supply chain disruptions, workforce management and regulatory restrictions, and prioritized operational efficiency, cost-saving initiatives and adapting to evolving circumstances. For more information on our assessment of private equity returns during three recent periods of market distress, view our paper The Historical Impact of Economic Downturns on Private Equity.

While almost half of managers in the survey are not expecting tariffs to impact EBITDA, and only 2% are expecting a significant impact, many private equity-backed companies are actively considering and undertaking mitigation measures. As part of our survey, we asked private equity managers which mitigation strategies their portfolio companies were considering implementing. Figure 5 shows which strategies were cited. Of the companies potentially impacted by tariffs, many of them are considering pursuing multiple measures. The most cited strategies are “increase prices”, “diversify suppliers or shift sourcing to non-tariffed countries” and “reduce operational expenses or costs”.

Figure 5: Percent Of Private Equity-backed Companies Considering Adopting Each Mitigation Strategy—PE Manager Survey Data

Private Equity-backed Companies Considering Building/Increasing U.S. Production

We asked private equity managers about their companies’ plans to either build or increase U.S. production or move to U.S.-based suppliers. As shown in figure 6, just 17% of managers said that their companies are considering such a move, including 4% planning to do so in the near term. This is notable, as almost half of managers are not expecting their companies’ EBITDA to be affected by tariffs.

Figure 6: Percent Of Managers Whose Companies Are Considering Building/increasing U.S. Production Or Moving To A U.S.-based Supplier—PE Manager Survey Data

Limited Expected Tariff Impact on Near-Term Portfolio Company Valuations

Understanding that each industry and company has unique exposure regarding potential tariffs, we asked private equity managers about near-term valuation expectations, a topic that is “top of mind” for many investors. As shown in figure 7, approximately 73% of private equity managers expect no near-term valuation changes as a result of the potential tariffs and approximately 27% expect a near-term valuation reduction of less than 25%. To note, no respondent predicted either an increase in valuations or a decrease in valuations by more than 25%.

Figure 7: Prediction Of Near-term Valuation Impact Of Tariffs—PE Manager Survey Data

Private Equity Managers are Well-Positioned to Capitalize on the Current Market Environment

We believe that private equity managers are well-equipped to capitalize on dislocation opportunities created by potential tariffs and their second-order economic effects, but recognize that the range of business and economic outcomes remains wide given the still-uncertain situation.

Private equity managers’ proactive, flexible and forward-thinking approach has historically allowed them to pivot strategies, leverage change and drive value creation in the face of dislocation in the market, as evidenced by their resilience during downturns like COVID-19 and the 2008 Financial Crisis.

While broader market uncertainty has clouded the near-term outlook for both dealmaking and exit activity, we believe that disruption could create interesting opportunities for well-positioned capital solutions providers.

Tariff-related developments remain fluid and subject to change. Neuberger Berman Private Markets remains committed to actively engaging with private equity managers to assess their perspectives on market dynamics and the potential implications of tariffs.
