data.title: Matson: A Quasi-Monopoly On Sale
data.description:
A shipping company operating their fleet exclusively on the pacific is bound to feel the pinch of tariffs and their consequences on trade flows. Matson (NYSE:MATX) is such a company, the stock is already down 40% from its all-time high. It sounds like an “easy pass”, but I will argue otherwise.

You see, more than half of Matson’s business is walled off and protected by a hundred-year-old U.S. Law (Merchant Marine Act of 1920 “Jones Act”). This makes it akin to a regulated monopoly, for the portion of their business that encompasses West Coast U.S. ports and Hawaii, Alaska and Guam.

The company has capitalized on their position, building the largest Jones Act fleet and achieving fantastic financial performance. The CAGRs on the usual financial metrics, since 2012:

Amazingly, it has managed to achieve this with almost no debt. A rarity in capital intensive shipowner business. In fact, if you consider their capital construction fund (CCF), then the company operates with no net debt. This is also visible in the interest income (earned mainly from those funds on the CCF invested in Treasuries) of $36 million and $48 million in 2023, and 2024 respectively vs. interest expense averaging $10 million per year.

Pacific Ocean Sailors

Matson operates 18 Jones Act-compliant vessels (17 owned, 1 chartered – a small barge). This means the ships were constructed in a U.S. shipyard and are crewed mostly by U.S. Sailors. In addition, the company owns and charters another 10 vessels, some of which are foreign flagged.

Connecting West Coast ports with Hawaii, Alaska and Guam is their main business. Consider these relatively (or dramatically, depending on the case) isolated economies, these shipping lanes are pipelines for goods that are too bulky or uneconomical to fly. Matson’s prospects are directly tied to the future growth of these economies, and the longevity of the Jones Act.

After ships offload in Hawaii and Guam, routes are in some cases extend to Japan and China. The China business has become an important contributor to margin. Keep in mind, these ships (U.S. manned) are not the most economical to run. They can compete in these routes because they are already “near” and empty. Diverting to other Asian ports to pick up cargo is a logical step before returning to the U.S.

The volume breakdowns are like this:

The China routes

It is very likely that most of the drop in the stock price is related to the anticipation and then ignition of the US-China trade dispute. Regardless of how this turns out, there will be some volatility and a likely reduction in trade.

Matson is building three of their larger “Aloha class” vessels in Philly. All three will be efficient, dual fuel (LNG and fuel oil), and Jones Act assets that will allow for more efficient service in their core markets. They will be delivered in 2027 and 2028. By then, we will have a lot more clarity on trade relationships between the two countries. I expect Matson to have flexibility to adjust to potentially slower China volumes by returning chartered ships when able.

As noted in the table above, China is roughly 33% of TEU volume, probably less by revenues. And no one reasonably expects trade flow to drop to zero, therefore, a drop in these lanes is not an existential risk for Matson. A repeal of the Jones Act is more critical, though.

The Joes Act

The century old law underpins Matson’s business model. Building ships in the U.S. is substantially more expensive. So are the crews and taxes of US flagged vessels. The Jones Act is not intended to minimize ocean freight, it is intended to sustain U.S. shipyards (and the Jobs they create) and ocean fairing crews.

The resulting “merchant marine”, although privately owned, can be leveraged in times of war. That is why a majority U.S. ownership is also a requirement under the law. And shipbuilders would be available to replenish a fighting, Navy. That is the reason behind the law.

There are detractors, of course. The offshore wind industry is constrained by the lack of specialized wind-mill construction vessels that can comply with the Act. During hurricanes and other emergencies, the Act is often waived to allow for fuel and essentials to be moved within the U.S. by non-flagged vessels. And of course, the cost of higher ocean freight is borne by the economies of Hawaii and Alaska (while the jobs are generated in the shipyard on the continental U.S.)

For owners of Matson, therefore, getting a feel for the political winds is a must. In their recent annual report, the company states the following:

“Matson believes that the geopolitical environment has further solidified political support for U.S. flagged vessels because a vital and dedicated U.S. merchant marine is a cornerstone for a strong homeland defense, as well as a critical source of trained U.S. mariners for wartime support”

This position is further supported by the following article on American Thinker, which was shared in December by President Trump. Maintaining the Act as it stands seems to fit well with his America first narrative. It is hard to see how letting shipbuilding go ties in with a manufacturing resurrection.

Valuation

Matson’s market cap is $3.4B, or $3B backing out net cash position (including treasuries in the CCF fund). Their main commitment is the $1B investment in the three Aloha class ships under construction, to be delivered in two and three years from now. The CCF is already funded with about 65% of the price tag, and milestone payment (mainly for steel purchases) have already started.

Because of that funded commitment, most of the EBITDA of around $700 million less capex of $300 million should accrue to owners. The company has paid a steady dividend and also regularly purchases shares in the open market. All this hints at quality capital management, and that is definitely the case. Their ROIC is very impressive:

The average is clearly affected by the post-pandemic supply chain crunch, that temporarily raised rates and volumes. Today’s level of 16% may be on the high side, as rates are still held up by Red Sea passage restrictions (tying up ships on longer routes, effectively reducing industry capacity).

Going forward, I expect ROIC to normalize around 12% to 14%, still a very high level considering most of that invested capital is equity. Given such quality results, and a regulatory protected market, you would expect high multiples on earnings…but that is not the case.

Clearly, the weight of the tariff rift and uncertainty around global trade flows - particularly on the China-U.S. corridor is hanging on the shares.

Already down almost 40% from a recent ATH, the market reaction has been swift. When considering the negligible debt levels, their ability to adjust the charter fleet and their future upgrades to that fleet (the three LNG Aloha Class vessels - already mostly funded), the future is not so cloudy.

What are the risks, then?

Aside from the major risk (which is political - a repeal or significant amendment to the Jones Act), the other risks are related to the markets they serve. Hawaii GDP growth, growth in tourism and construction all help bring and keep volumes high. In fact, the more manufacturing that happens in America, the more goods that Hawaii and Alaska would purchase within Jones Act waters (but all of that will take months and years to materialize).

In the meantime, lower volume of goods from China will be replaced with… what? Maybe other Asian nations like S. Korea, or Vietnam once they reach deals with the administration.

It is difficult to know how supply will adjust, but there are some things we do know: i) demand for sneakers, iPhones, clothes, TVs may diminish some but will not go away and ii) factories will not cross oceans overnight. Re-shoring will take time, the outsourcing wave is decades in the making.

Conclusion

It takes some getting used to investing in a company that is built around a particular regulatory quirk. It seems most of the value could potentially go away in one swoop.

On the other hand, the Jones Act has survived a century of challenges. It does have support from both sides and at the moment from the executive as well. The risk of the Act going away can be seen as the sword of Damocles; until we remember that all other shipping companies have to compete for servicing other routes.

A repeal of the Act would mean Matson could hire non U.S. nationals and buy ships from other shipowners. Their sunk cost - no pun intended - on higher priced vessels is not an impediment to compete (it would indirectly if it had caused a high debt load, but that is not the case).

Even such a worst-case scenario would not sink the company. Their vessels are part of the industry fleet and could be put to use in wherever routes demand the capacity. The likelihood of this happening is low, and greater geopolitical conflict only makes the likelihood lower still.

In the meantime, we can expect superior economics and superior returns from a protected market to keep their heads above water.

data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs: Hope Is Not A Strategy
data.description:
The idea of what a business can be and what the reality is have to merge at some point. While the best investments are often in companies and industries that are facing tough times, management teams have to eventually produce real and tangible results that show actual progress toward meaningful goals.

Cleveland-Cliffs' (NYSE:CLF) CEO Lourenco Goncalves has talked a lot since becoming CEO in 2014 in addition to aggressively using capital to make acquisitions and initiate massive share buybacks.

Cliffs has offered investors total returns of just 46.2 percent over the last decade, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of nearly 206 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about this integrated steel company in August 2024, and I was negative on Cliffs primarily because of my bearish view on the flat and hot coiled steel market in North America, as well as Goncalves' use of capital to focus too much on aggressive share buybacks. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating on Cleveland-Cliffs. The recent run-up in hot rolled steel prices because of the tariffs Trump enacted in February is not likely sustainable for multiple reasons, with rates likely to remain elevated and auto demand continuing to be weak. The company's recent Stelco acquisition and partnership with Nucor (NUE) to try to purchase U.S. Steel (X) are also not likely to produce meaningful results for shareholders. Cliffs still looks overvalued.

Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest producer of flat-rolled and automotive-grade steel in the United States. Hot-rolled and coated steel account for just over 60% of what the company sells, with nearly 31% of their end steel products sold to automotive manufacturers.

Hot rolled and coiled steel prices in North America have been in a general decline since hitting record highs in 2021 coming out of the pandemic, as supply was unable to keep up with pent-up demand. Still, as new supply came online, and growth rates stabilized, prices predictably fell significantly in late 2022 and early 2023. While hot rolled steel prices have risen for shorter periods of time over the last 3 years, the market has generally been well-supplied, and these increases have proved unsustainable.

The recent increase in North American hot rolled and coiled steel prices won't likely change the overall long-term dynamic of this well-supplied market either.

US Steel production in February dropped to nearly 6000 thousand tons as the economy weakened, but since the rate of capability utilization in this sector is only near 74.4 percent, new production could be ramped fairly easily.

The increase in steel prices in the US as a result of Trump's tariffs was not because of rising demand, steel prices globally remain depressed.

The steel tariffs Trump enacted in 2018 also did not change supply or pricing in this market over the long term. While the President enacted larger and further reaching tariffs in February, Trump has also stated that this action was part of a larger strategy to renegotiate trade deals with the US's economic partners. The steel market also has the capacity to adjust in the long run to this likely temporary disruption, and new supplies should lead to prices falling again. The futures market is currently pricing hot-rolled and coiled steel prices to fall by 20 percent from current levels by September to nearly $800 per short ton.

North American steel prices should also remain weak over an extended amount of time as higher rates lower demand for new autos, the used car market remains very strong, with sales in this industry rising 16 percent in February. Consumer spending numbers also continue to disappoint, and construction spending is expected to slow significantly in 2025 and 2026 as the building of nonresidential structures is expected to fall. There is also notable new expected production in this flat-rolled steel market that is being planned as well.

Cleveland Cliff's operating margins remain near the bottom of the steel industry, and the company has cut capital expenditures to generate more cash flow to pay for massive buybacks that have not produced returns for shareholders. The company's net margins have fallen for 5 consecutive quarters now.

While the company's recent acquisition of Stelco was accretive to fourth-quarter earnings and Cliffs saw cost reductions of $30 a ton on a year-to-year basis, the integrated steel producer still has industry-low margins.

This is why Cleveland-Cliffs still looks overvalued at 8.58x expected forward cash flow and 34x trailing cash flow. The company has cut capital expenditures to generate more cash flow, but the company has still been unable to reach the steel producer's previously stated goal of being debt-free, and Cliff's industry-low margins are in part the result of underinvestment in the core business over a number of years. The massive share buybacks authorized have also just largely left shareholders with more debt. Cliff's partnership with Nucor to purchase US Steel, which faces significant regulatory hurdles, is also primarily a defensive action to keep Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY) from being able to undercut the company's pricing.

All investment theses have risk, and Trump is very loyal to this industry that employs thousands of American workers and also helped him get elected. The President is likely to continue to use tariffs and other actions to protect the US steel industry, as he did on a smaller scale in 2018. If the Fed were to aggressively cut rates or the economy were to strengthen, that would obviously also likely increase demand for new autos. Still, Trump isn't likely to keep the current massive tariffs he imposed in place longer-term.

Goncalves has now been the CEO of Cleveland-Cliffs for over a decade, and I think his aggressive approach of focusing on massive share buybacks and significant acquisitions has not produced for shareholders. While Trump is likely to continue to take actions to protect the US Steel industry, the underlying dynamic in this sector is likely to continue to remain unfavorable for Cliffs as well.

data.title: PepsiCo: Decreasing Sales Volumes Are A Short-Term Trend
data.description:
PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP) is a company I have recently added to my portfolio. They are a dividend king, with a 52-year track record of increases. Over the past year, the company is down by 12.10%, up by only 6.03% over the past 5 years, meaning that they have heavily underperformed the SPY500, which through the same time period is up by 89.95%. In this analysis, to understand why I rate the company as a BUY, we will first go through the market overview to figure out why Pepsi's stock price is falling. We will also have a look at the latest acquisition of Poppi, to then move to the fundamental 9 Pillar Analysis, and finally calculate the company's intrinsic value through 4 of our valuation models.

Introduction

Before moving to the analysis, let's introduce the business. As many of you know, PepsiCo sells both beverages and food/snacks, both in the US and internationally. In 2024, snacks accounted for 58.00% of the company's revenue, with beverages accounting for 42.00%. 60.00% of sales came from NA, and 40.00% came from abroad.

Analysis

Market Overview

Now, what I want to point out is that sales from all three segments have been growing. International sales have been increasing at a 5Y CAGR of 10.00%. The PBNA (PepsiCo Beverages North America) segment has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 7.00%, and the PFNA (PepsiCo Foods North America) has been growing at a CAGR of 4.00%.

Now, what we have to understand is that although the revenue has been growing, it hasn't been growing for the best of reasons. With rising inflation through the past few years, customers have been buying less and less treats (sodas and snacks), leading to overall fewer units being sold. PepsiCo has been making some steep retail price hikes to their products to counter this, which is what has been keeping the steady revenue growth alive.

So as you can see, this is something many investors are worried about. The company's financials are growing, but due to increasing retail prices rather than increasing volume sales. Of course, this is even more troublesome when we realize that the company won't be able to increase the retail prices forever, which would lead to their revenue falling and following closely behind the stock price falling.

Now, if you are a long-term investor, you probably do not have that much to fear. As I have already said, consumers have been cutting back on spendings due to high inflation through the past few years, which is what is causing the falling sales volumes. Through the next decades, I do expect inflation to turn back to more normalized levels, where it has been sitting for most of the past 35 years.

This would, of course, mean that customers would turn back to buying similar amounts of snacks and beverages as before the inflation hit.

Before looking at the recent Poppi acquisition, I want to mention one more thing that supports my long-term BUY rating. As I have already mentioned, 60.00% of the company's sales come from NA, which accounts for only 4.80% of the total global population. This means that PepsiCo has an opportunity to grow their geographical portfolio, especially as, in the long term, Third World countries, which currently contribute the most to the world's population growth, become more developed.

Poppi Acquisition

Regarding the very recent acquisition, it is something I find as a great positive. Poppi is a prebiotic soda brand, which resonates with customers' rising concerns regarding healthy living. The acquisition will cost PepsiCo $1.95B, which of course will probably increase their debt levels. This though doesn't bother me that much, as for Pepsi, which generated more than $7.00B in FCF in 2024, a $2.00B investment that could greatly boost the brand's performance is a small price to pay. Furthermore, it shows that PepsiCo is committed to innovating and retaining its position as the market leader. This is further supported by their 2022 partnership with Celsius, a company that is converting a large percentage of the energy drink market to its customers by offering sugar-free "healthier" energy drinks. Overall, I believe that PepsiCo is on a great track, acquiring smaller companies that might bring a lot to the table in the future as the energy drink and snack market shifts to a healthier lifestyle.

9 Pillar Analysis

But now that we have established what is going on with the beverage and snack market both in the short and long term, let's get to analyze the company's fundamentals, and see if they are indeed a company worth buying and holding.

For those of you who aren't familiar with the 9 Pillars, let me quickly sum them up. Pillars 1 and 2 represent the 5-year Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and Price-to-Free Cash Flow (P/FCF) ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 illustrate the growth in revenue, net income, and free cash flow over the past 5 years. Pillar 6 focuses on the 5-year trailing profitability, while Pillar 7 highlights the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 tracks dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the ratio of long-term liabilities to 5-year free cash flow minus dividends, which assesses a company's capability to pay down debt within a reasonable period while still paying dividends. Below, you can see that PepsiCo gets a rating of 6/9.

Debt and Dividends (Pillars 8 and 9)

Firstly, let's take a look at the elephant in the room, and that is the company's debt levels. Pepsi's LTL/5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting extremely high, at 17.75, which means that while paying out their current dividend, with the average FCF the company generates, it would take them more than 75 years to pay off all their LTL. To put this into perspective, the company currently has $48.17B in LTL, which isn't that much compared to the 5Y average FCF of $6.82B they generate yearly. The issue is that their 5Y FCF payout ratio is sitting very high, at 92.15%, and in 2024, it sat at 100.56%, which means that the company had to go into debt to pay out their dividend.

Of course, PepsiCo is a dividend king with a steady 5Y dividend CAGR of 7.30%, and a yield of 3.69%, which, in fact, is the highest it has been over the past 10 years due to the recent sell-off. This, though, doesn't soften the fact that they are heavily in debt. What makes the situation better though is that if there were to come financial hardship, which very well might happen in the short term, the company could work with their dividend payouts to afford more debt. Furthermore, they have been repurchasing a lot of debt recently, lowering it by 9.26% through the past 5 years.

Overall, I don't mind the high LTL too much from the perspective of it hurting the company because, as we can see, they can afford it. What I do mind, though, is that it will take a lot of value off of the table for investors, as the debt will have to be paid down, and therefore, the company won't be able to return the $48.17B as value to investors.

Talking about returning value to investors, another thing I want to point out is that with such a high FCF payout ratio and LTL, the company has almost no FCF to repurchase shares. To put this into perspective, through the past 5Y, PepsiCo has repurchased only 1.01% of their overall shares outstanding, which accounts for a low CAGR of 0.20%.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

Regardless, let's now take a look at the financial growth. First, looking to the revenue, that has grown by 30.53% through the past 5Y, growing at a decent CAGR of 5.59%, from $70.37B in 2020 to $91.85B in 2024. Although this CAGR would seem very decent for a company like Pepsi, something worth mentioning is that their growth has been decreasing. In 2021, their revenue increased by 12.93%, in 2022 by 8.70%, in 2023 by 5.88%, and in 2024 by only 0.42%. So it is quite obvious that the growth is decreasing at a high rate, and in the short term, with the inflation and tariff uncertainty looming, this trend might continue.

Nevertheless, moving to the net income, which has increased from $7.12B in 2020, to $9.58B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 6.27%. Similarly, in this time frame, the EPS has grown by 35.75%, growing at a CAGR of 6.46%, so slightly faster than the net income.

Finally, looking at the FCF, that has increased from $6.37B in 2020, to $7.19B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 4.40%, and a low 5Y increase of only 12.80%.

Overall, it could be said that the company has been growing, but the growth has been decreasing, and accordingly, investors have been becoming more wary of the situation, lowering the company's stock price as expectations start facing reality.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Before we get to calculating the intrinsic value, let's take a look at the 5Y PE and P/FCF ratios. The 5Y PE is sitting at 24.29, higher than the current PE of 21.12, meaning that the company is currently cheaper compared to what they were trading at 5 years ago, which again might be justified by short-term concerns regarding decreasing sales volumes.

The P/FCF is currently sitting quite high at 28.11, which is slightly lower than the 5Y average of 29.86, but in my eyes, is still quite high for a low-growth company such as Pepsi.

Overall, in my eyes, the 9 Pillars are not looking good, but neither are they looking bad. The company has to an extent been growing their financials and is currently cheaper than 5 years ago compared to the net income they bring in. Although they do have high debt, they generate enough FCF to cover it if things were to get really bad. One thing I love is the steady dividends, which though might start increasing at a much lower CAGR through the next years as the company's FCF growth slows down and their LTL deadlines get closer.

Intrinsic Value

Nevertheless, now that we have gone through the 9 Pillars, let's calculate the company's intrinsic value, first using the Graham's Formula.

Here we will take the company's 5Y EPS sitting at $6.53. I will then predict a future CAGR of 5.33% and arrive at an intrinsic value of only $87.05, 40.68% lower than the current trading price of $146.75.

For the second model, we will look at the DCF model, where I will take the 5Y average FCF and, based on that, calculate the future FCF for the next 10Y + using a CAGR of 4.70%. After discounting all of this FFCF at a rate of 7.00%, we will get an intrinsic value of $98.28, which from a large part is this low due to the high debt of $47.75B, accounting for more than 25.00% of all the company's FFCF.

For the Multiples Valuation, I will compare Pepsi's PE to the PE of Coca-Cola, and get an intrinsic value of $204.69 after multiplying Coca-Cola's PE with the EPS of PepsiCo sitting at $6.95.

Finally, looking to the DDM, I will project a lower than the historical dividend CAGR into the future of 4.00% and get an intrinsic value of $187.89.

After averaging out the four values we calculated, we will get an ultimate intrinsic value of $144.48, which is 1.55% lower than the current trading price of $146.75. After applying a safety margin of 10.00%, our acceptable buying price would be at $130.03.

Conclusion

In conclusion, regardless of PepsiCo's intrinsic value with an applied margin of safety coming out at $130.03, I do have to rate the company as a BUY. This rating is as due to the company's long-term potential, supported by their position in the market and willingness to innovate. Even though in the short term they might face struggles, I believe that when the recession falls to normalized levels, the company's revenue growth will continue as normal. Regarding the fundamentals, I wouldn't be too concerned about the LTL as in the worst-case scenario, the company would be forced to cut their dividend. PepsiCo is, in my eyes, a bulletproof stock that will bring investors great value, mainly in the form of steady dividends rather than high future growth, and currently, they are the cheapest they have been through the past 5 years.

data.title: Immunome: Transformed Pipeline Offers Multiple Catalysts
data.description:
Not every biotech investor knows Immunome (NASDAQ:IMNM), but I see their unique approach to antibody discovery coupled with their strategic pipeline expansion as a compelling opportunity in oncology. A combination of factors drives my bullish stance: An unparalleled human immune memory-based discovery engine, an asset pipeline moving critical assets into & through the clinic, and an undervalued valuation that I think underestimates the long-term potential - particularly after its dramatic merger with Morphimmune.

Financial Position

A biotech lives and dies by its cash runway. Immunome looks well-capitalized for the near to medium-term following the merger financing and subsequent January 2025 financing round. As reported in its full year 2024 financial results, the company had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $217.3 million as of December 31, 2024. Add the $172.5 million raised in January 2025 for a cash balance of $390 million.

Running costs reflect the company's active clinical development. Research and development expenses amounted to $129.5 million for the full year 2024, alongside $152.3 million categorized as in-process R&D (mainly relating to the Morphimmune acquisition), and $33.0 million for general and administrative expenses. The reported net loss for 2024 was $293 million driven by acquisition-related costs and higher R&D investments. Based on the improved cash position following recent financing, Immunome projects that its cash runway will last through 2027, providing sufficient capital to fund varegacestat Phase 3 data readouts and expansion of ADC/RLT programs into early clinical studies.

The Discovery Platform

The key to Immunome's advantage is its discovery platform. Not using computational design or animal immunization alone, the company directly isolates and analyzes memory B cells from human subjects that have produced an immune response. The premise is elegant: The human body has trained these B cells to recognize and target disease-specific antigens - including cancer cells. Interrogating this natural library, Immunome seeks to identify fully human antibodies with potentially better targeting capabilities and therapeutic profiles. I think this biological insight gives it an edge in finding novel targets and effective antibody candidates and may reduce downstream development risk compared to more artificial discovery methods. While true testing will only occur with clinical validation, the underlying principle is sound and forms the basis of the company's pipeline.

Pipeline Overview

With the Morphimmune merger and subsequent strategic decisions, the Immunome pipeline focused more on targeted oncology modalities such as ADCs and RLTs than just antibody expertise.

Now leading the charge is IM-1021, an ROR1-targeted ADC. The target ROR1 is highly expressed on many hematological and solid tumors but absent from normal adult tissues, making it attractive for targeted therapies. Immunome reported encouraging preclinical data for IM-1021 in late 2024 demonstrating potent anti-tumor activity in liquid and solid tumor models with a topoisomerase I linker-payload optimized for efficacy and possibly enhanced safety. Critically, the FDA cleared the Investigational New Drug application for IM-1021 in late 2024 and the first patient was dosed in the Phase 1 trial in March 2025. This quick transition from preclinical validation to clinical initiation illustrates the execution capabilities of the team.

The other major asset received via Morphimmune is IM-3050, a Fibroblast Activation Protein (FAP) targeted RLT. As a marker of tumor microenvironment expression, FAP is expressed in the tumor microenvironment of many cancers and represents another promising route to targeted delivery, with a radioactive payload. In March 2025, Immunome filed the IND for IM-3050 and expects to initiate a Phase 1 trial this year.

The ADC and RLT programs are now prominently placed, but the previously highlighted anti-IL-38 antibody program IM-4240 (not to be confused with ROR1 ADC IM-1021) remains an intriguing prospect although its IND timeline looks less immediate. It is an IL-1 family cytokine with multiple roles in inflammation and immunity. Though usually considered anti-inflammatory, recent findings may also be pro-tumorigenic in some cancers, thereby suppressing anti-tumor immune responses. Studies linked higher IL-38 expression to a worse prognosis in lung cancer and breast cancer, with reduced T-cell infiltration. Blockade of IL-38 shows preclinical promise for activating anti-tumor immunity. So it appears that the IND submission for this program, earlier expected, now appears further out, probably deprioritized relative to ADC/RLT assets but potentially valuable nonetheless.

It also includes varegacestat (formerly AL102), a gamma secretase inhibitor from Ayala Pharmaceuticals. Varegacestat is undergoing Phase 3 (RINGSIDE NCT04871282) for desmoid tumors - a rare, non-cancerous but locally aggressive tumor type. Final full enrollment closed in February 2024, and topline data is expected in the second half of 2025. Positive Phase 3 data may provide Immunome with its first potential path to market, albeit in a niche indication. Above and beyond these named candidates, Immunome is still leveraging its discovery engine and ADC expertise with preclinical ADCs IM-1617, IM-1335, and IM-1340 targeting unknown solid tumor antigens that are in the IND-enabling manufacturing stages.

The Morphimmune Merger

A merger agreement between Immunome and Morphimmune was closed later in the year under this announcement in June 2023. They combined Immunome's B cell discovery capabilities with Morphimmune's targeted therapy platform and expertise. For Morphimmune stockholders, Immunome common stock was received, resulting in pre-financing ownership of approximately 55% for Immunome security holders and 45% for Morphimmune security holders.

Parallel to that deal, the companies secured $125 million in private placement financing for the integrated pipeline. This combination created a more diversified entity with expertise in multiple therapeutic modalities and a strengthened leadership team with experience in ADC development and commercialization - Siegall was at Seagen. This new combined company kept the Immunome name and ticker, with headquarters in Seattle and research sites in Pennsylvania and Indiana.

Recent Developments

The start of the Phase 1 trial for the ROR1 ADC IM-1021 with the first patient dosed in March 2025 marks a significant step towards human testing of this promising candidate. Equally important was the March 2025 IND submission for the FAP-targeted RLT IM-3050, which will allow another novel targeted therapy to enter clinical evaluation later in the year.

It is not breaking news that the varegacestat Phase 3 RINGSIDE trial is proceeding as planned, with full enrollment completed a year ago and topline data readout expected in the second half of 2025 with potential near-term value inflection. Moreover, it secured $172.5 million in additional financing through January 2025.

Risks and Competition

The greatest obstacle is clinical trial risk. Preclinical promise rarely translates into human efficacy and safety. Both IM-1021 and IM-3050 are just entering Phase 1, historically the stage with the highest attrition rates, while varegacestat, although in Phase 3, targets a difficult-to-treat condition. Any trial failure, safety concern, or major delay would skew the company's valuation. Beyond clinical risk is regulatory risk, as getting FDA approval is long and never guaranteed, despite seemingly positive data. The competition also creates problems. The targets in oncology, especially ADC and RLT, are very crowded and include several companies targeting the same targets as ROR1 and FAP. Competitors could achieve clinical success or regulatory approval more quickly, or their therapies could demonstrate superior efficacy or safety profiles, limiting Immunome's market potential. Furthermore, although the cash runway looks robust through 2027, development stages including potential late-stage trials for IM-1021 or IM-3050 and eventual commercialization will require significant additional capital and could result in shareholder dilution through future financing rounds. Then there is platform risk; Though the memory B cell discovery engine seems technically interesting, its long-term ability to consistently generate successful clinical candidates at scale remains to be demonstrated.

Valuation and Market Context

Without revenue and large R&D investments, a clinical-stage biotech like Immunome could not be valued on traditional metrics. I believe a risk-adjusted view of its lead candidates' total addressable market (TAM) is more relevant, given the high degree of uncertainty.

Now let us focus on the two lead targeted therapy assets entering the clinic: IM-1021 (ROR1 ADC) & IM-3050 (FAP RLT). Among other cancers, ROR1 expression is expressed in certain leukemias, such as lymphomas and solid tumors like triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), ovarian and lung cancers. TNBC affects 15-20% of breast cancer patients. With roughly 300,000 new US breast cancer cases annually, that translates to 45,000-60,000 cases of TNBC. With ROR1 expressed in 20-30% of these, the target US population subset could be 10,000-18,000 annually for TNBC alone. Including other potential ROR1+tumors would greatly expand this pool. FAP, targeted by IM-3050, is expressed in the tumor stroma of pancreatic, colorectal, breast, and lung cancers. Though difficult to define precisely, its distribution across major tumor types suggests a potential patient pool of the hundreds of thousands in key markets, especially later treatment lines.

With regard to pricing, current ADCs range between $100,000 and over $250,000 $per patient annually, and targeted radiotherapies are also very expensive. A conservative estimate of $150,000 per patient per year for a successful Immunome therapy yields a speculative peak sales calculation. In terms of IM-1021, capturing 15% of a hypothetical 40,000 patients in the US/EU5 ROR1 + market could generate $900 million annually. Just 10% of the larger hypothetical 80,000 patients FAP + market could bring in $1.2 billion annually for IM-3050.

Even at large risk discounts, appropriate for early-stage assets (potentially 70-80% or more), the potential risk-adjusted value derived from these markets could be much greater than its market capitalization today.

Conclusion

Immunome presents a typical investment profile for biotech, but with some distinguishing characteristics. This gives the memory cell discovery platform an unusual vantage point to find potent, targeted antibodies. Combined with experienced leadership, the strategic integration of Morphimmune's ADC and RLT capabilities has resulted in a targeted oncology pipeline for validated pathways such as ROR1 and FAP. Having two key assets entering Phase 1 trials and one nearing data readout, the company sees multiple potential catalysts over the next 1-3 years.

Fortunately, the financial runway looks solid into 2027, which helps with immediate financing concerns. Although the main risk remains clinical failure, the current valuation when weighed against the substantial TAM of its lead programs suggests that the market is overly discounting the success possibility. For the risk-tolerant investors with a long-term view, Immunome represents a unique opportunity to invest in a potentially transformative new paradigm for cancer therapy, leveraging human immune insights for new weapons against cancer.

data.title: DocuSign: Rule Of 40 In Sight And $130 On The Horizon, I'm Long
data.description:
Investment Thesis

DocuSign (NASDAQ:DOCU), isn't the business you remember from the Covid years. I get that. But at the same time, it's not priced as such.

What's more, I make the case that during this tariff war, this SaaS business is largely immune from the likely fallout of tariff implications. I explain this further in the analysis.

On top of that, fundamentally, DocuSign is close to the rule of 40% (growth plus FCF margin equals 38%).

In short, this is my declaration that DocuSign is very attractively priced at 12x forward free cash flow. What's more, its billings, which are a leading indicator of its future growth rate, are expected to move higher this year. Reversing the course of 5 years of shrinking billings.

Hence, my price target of $130 per share by summer 2026.

Important Context

The table that follows shows that DocuSign makes about 70% of its revenues from the US.

This gets to the crux of my argument. Investors right now are navigating an extremely fluid environment with recession fears, as well as tariff uncertainty.

Meanwhile, I argue that the market has already suffered a great deal. Yes, there's a lot of bad news circling around, and there's a lot of uncertainty, unquestionably. But at the same time, who doesn't know this already?

As a result, I believe that backing SaaS businesses that make the majority of their revenues from the US, will be better placed to navigate this period.

Plus, thus far, the majority of the tariffs appear to be aimed at supplies being imported to the US, with services not being targeted.

Consequently, I believe that this doesn't necessarily alone make backing software names the perfect investment, since there's still likely to be recession concerns, as well as potentially a higher inflation, which would lead to higher interest rates, but on balance, I believe that the market is unjustifiably pushing these businesses.

DocuSign's Near-Term Prospects

DocuSign makes it easy for people and businesses to sign documents electronically instead of dealing with paper and printers.

When you're signing a contract, DocuSign helps you get it done securely from anywhere. The real value? It speeds up processes and makes sure everything is legally binding— without errors.

Looking ahead, DocuSign is betting big on something called Intelligent Agreement Management.

Instead of just signing documents, IAM helps businesses manage agreements using AI.

It can pull key insights from contracts to automate workflows. In the short term, Intelligent Agreement Management is growing fast, especially with small and mid-sized businesses, and DocuSign is working to get bigger companies on board too.

In sum, DocuSign has built a strong brand as the go-to name for e-signatures and is pushing hard with its Intelligent Agreement Management growth opportunity to end up doing more than just e-signing.

Given this context, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Guided for Around 6% Y/Y

Billings are a leading indicator of revenue growth rates. Billings for fiscal year 2026 are expected to be up 9% y/y. This is the first time that billings are going to accelerate since fiscal 2021.

So, even though revenues are being guided for 6% y/y revenue growth rates, the key here is that billings are expected to translate into revenues in the later stages of fiscal 2026, which should mean an acceleration of its revenue growth rates in the back end of this fiscal year and into early next year, fiscal 2027 (starts in February 2026).

DOCU Stock Valuation – 12x Forward Free Cash Flow

I believe that you should invest, thinking of the worst-case scenario. You should always be mentally prepared for the worst-case scenario.

And yet, my contention is this: if you don't buy stocks on sale, then when do you buy them? And given that stocks only go on sale when there's bad news, and there's no other reason for stocks to go on sale, if this isn't close to worst-case scenario already, then what is?

With DocuSign holding no debt, this removes a lot of worst-case scenarios off the table. Realistically, a business with no debt can't go bankrupt! So, what we are left with is a question mark over its medium-term valuation.

However, given that DocuSign holds approximately $950 million of cash and cash equivalents, not including its more than $100 million of long-term investments, this provides the business with ample flexibility. Case in point, roughly 6% of its market cap is made up of cash and marketable securities.

On top of that, I believe that at some point in the next twelve months, DocuSign will deliver $1.3 billion of free cash flow.

Here's my math.

  • DocuSign's management states its free cash flow margin should be roughly 250 basis points higher than its non-GAAP operating margin, consistent with the prior year.
  • Last year, they delivered 30% non-GAAP operating margins, which ended up as just under 32% free cash flow margins.
  • This year, the guidance points to 29%, which, I believe, given the company's momentum, could end up at 30% to 31% non-GAAP operating margins.
  • This would reinforce that around 33% free cash flow margins could show up this year, which means as much as $1.3 billion of free cash flow on the cards.

Again, it doesn't necessarily matter too much if it's $1.1 billion or $1.3 billion that shows up in fiscal 2026, as much as there's a path to $1.3 billion of free cash flow, and that free cash flow can be counted on to grow further in the next several years.

Recall, in the PEG ratio, I use free cash flow, rather than earnings. And in this case, the stock is priced at 12x divided by 6 for its growth rate, which equals 2x. With anything less than 3x on the PEG ratio being viewed as an attractive valuation.

Risk Factors to Consider

The main risk factor here is how long until DocuSign can translate its higher billing growth rates of 9% to 10% y/y, into high single-digit revenue growth rates?

Could it be the case that it takes longer than expected for those billings to be reflected in its fundamentals?

If its billings figures were to decelerate to less than 7% y/y, I would be wrong on my thesis of this being a stable growth business.

Instead, I would have to abandon this business, as it was a business had just made a lot of noise about a single strong quarter of potential future growth, but there's not a lot of sustainability in these accelerating revenue growth rates.

The other risk front and center here is obvious. If inflation turns out to be climbing higher than expected, this will force the Fed to pivot towards higher interest rates, which would substantially damage the premium that investors are willing to pay for DocuSign.

The Bottom Line

I'm bullish on DocuSign because it is largely insulated from the ongoing tariff war, and its numbers just make sense.

The business is guiding for improved billings, up 9% y/y, which is the first sign of acceleration in years, and that's a big deal.

Also, financially, it's lean, with no debt and nearly $1 billion in cash, and it's trading at just 12x forward free cash flow.

If management can hit their margin targets, I see a clear path to $1.3 billion in free cash flow by fiscal 2026. That’s why I believe $130 per share by summer 2026 is within reach.
